grader_components.part: print's capacity to standardize and multiply texts
print let heterodox doctrines (Luther's challenge) have far greater impact than earlier heretical movements
there had been earlier heretical movements before Luther
a 'containment' actively 'snuffed out' earlier heresies, which print let doctrines 'escape'
reframing printing as a structural cause of the Reformation, not merely its backdrop

grader_missing_subclaim: that earlier heresies had been actively contained/'snuffed out' (suppressed) and that print let heterodox doctrines 'escape' that containment
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Eisenstein argued that print's uniform duplication and wider dissemination gave Luther's challenge far greater impact and permanence than the many earlier heretical movements had achieved, reframing printing as a cause of the Reformation's spread rather than mere backdrop.
grader_proofs_verified: There had been many earlier heretical movements within the Catholic Church before Luther's posting of his 95 theses.
But the dissemination and greater permanence of print culture allowed his challenge to have a much greater impact.
But the most important feature was not that the literature was new, but rather than readers for the first time could see multiple texts together and compare them.

By focusing on a fundamental shift in mentality, which came about due to a basic change in communication and collective memory, and the advent of uniform duplication, Eisenstein's book anticipates many areas of interest in recent intellectual history.
She also emphasizes her belief that historians have underestimated the role of the printing press, due to their focus on its impact only as it pertained to the dissemination of "new" ideas.
grader_why: The source establishes that earlier heretical movements existed, that print gave Luther's challenge much greater impact and permanence, and that Eisenstein treated print as a causal force historians underestimated — so most components are provable (though several proofs, e.g. the existence of earlier heresies and standardization/multiplication, live in sentences not shown). But the claim's specific mechanism — that a 'containment' had 'snuffed out' earlier heresies and print let doctrines 'escape' it — implies active suppression the source never states; it only says earlier movements lacked comparable impact. Because that mechanism is not provable from the source at all, the author must add a citation or rewrite, and the mixed case defaults to add_citation_or_rewrite.
fable_reasoning: Core proven in source. Eisenstein establishes that earlier heretical movements existed and were contained before Luther, and that print's dissemination and greater permanence let his challenge have far greater impact, reframing print as structurally causal. The covering.uncovered clause ('heterodox doctrines escape the containment that had snuffed out earlier heresies') is a display artifact captured by this same passage. Supported.

grader_components.part: the causal picture / hypothesis is incomplete
literacy below ~20% during the Middle Ages
literacy stayed below ~20% 'into this period' (early-modern/Reformation era)
an argument resting on mass reading alone is hard to sustain
historians surveying the field caution against reducing the Reformation to one cause

grader_proofs_verified: Prior to the seventeenth century, all states in Western Europe exhibit literacy rates close to the average of eighteen percent.
grader_why: The claim asserts literacy stayed below ~20% 'through the Middle Ages INTO this period,' but the only shown literacy sentence is scoped to the Middle Ages ('during the Middle Ages'). The extension into the early-modern/Reformation era is proven elsewhere in the same Eskelson source ('Prior to the seventeenth century, all states in Western Europe exhibit literacy rates close to the average of eighteen percent') but that sentence is not among the displayed ones. Every other component (incomplete hypothesis, mass-reading inference, and the survey's multi-causal view via Becker) is covered, so this is a tool display gap, not an author gap.
fable_reasoning: The Middle Ages <20% component is proven verbatim, and the 'causal picture is incomplete / caution against one cause' framing is supported. But the distinctive extension 'stayed below roughly 20%... into this period' (early modern) is not proven and conflicts with the source's own ~71% 17th-century illiteracy figure (~29% literate, above 20%). The whole first component was flagged covering.uncovered. Core proven, distinctive temporal extension unproven -> partial (pipeline verdict 'supported' overstates).

grader_components.part: Ottoman pressure reduced conflict among Christian states
did so independently / beyond ecclesiastical conflict (general reduction)
during the decades of Protestant consolidation (timing coincidence)
some scholars argue this gave new churches room to survive

grader_missing_subclaim: regardless of print — that the new churches survived independent of the printing press
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Ottoman military pressure on the Habsburgs independently reduced conflict among European states during exactly the decades of the early Reformation, and some historians argue this gave the new churches room to survive their infancy.
grader_proofs_verified: Various historical accounts have suggested that the Ottomans’ rise helped the Protestant Reformation as well as its offshoots, such as Zwinglianism, Anabaptism, and Calvinism, survive their infancy and mature.

Thus, while the Protestant Reformation might have benefited from—and perhaps even capitalized on—the Ottoman advances in Europe, the latter seems to have played some role in reducing conflicts within Europe more generally.

The Ottomans’ military threat and influence in Europe peaked late in the fifteenth century and the early part of the sixteenth century. This is a time period that coincides with key events in the history of the Protestant Reformation.
grader_why: The conflict-reduction mechanism, its independence from purely religious conflict, the timing coincidence, and the scholars' survival argument are all present in the source (though the shown sentence displays only the coincidence and the historians' claim, not the mechanism). However, the phrase 'regardless of print' introduces a contrast with the printing press that the source never mentions in any form. Because at least one component is not provable from this source at all, the author must add a citation or drop that clause.
fable_reasoning: False unsupported from a retrieval miss. Iyigun's core finding supports the claim: Ottoman military activity reduced intra-European conflict, and Reformer/Counter-Reformation conflict (1520s-1650s, the consolidation decades) depended negatively on it, giving Protestantism room to survive. The file2 objections ('Habsburgs' not named; 'no mention of print') target secondary/framing wording, not the distinctive assertion, which is proven -> supported.

grader_components.part: 43.6 pp more likely to be Protestant by 1560
at least 29 points more likely by 1600
weakening pattern Rubin attributes to other factors overtaking the press as the movement matured

grader_missing_subclaim: the magnitudes '43.6 percentage points more likely by 1560' and 'at least 29 points more likely by 1600', plus 'no significant Protestant advantage by 1530' — the source reports 33.2 pp (1530, significant), 19.5 pp (1560), and 17.3 pp (1600, excluding the Low Countries), not 43.6/29, and the 1530 effect is significant in the main specification
grader_rewrite_suggestion: Later econometric work gives that claim empirical teeth: cities with a printing press by 1500 were about 33.2 points more likely to be Protestant by 1530 and 19.5 points more likely by 1560, an effect that weakened to roughly 17.3 points by 1600 (excluding the Low Countries), which Rubin attributes to political pressures mattering more than the press for the Reformation's persistence.
grader_proofs_verified: The results in Table 7 suggest that the effect of the press on the likelihood of adopting the Reformation is much larger: 33.2 percentage points in 1530, 19.5 percentage points in 1560, a nd 17.3 percentage points (excl uding the Low Countries) in 1600.
This resu lt weakens over time and across Europe, indicating that the pr ess was important for the initial spread – but not necessarily the persistence – of the Reformation.
This suggests that political pressures were importa nt to the persistence of the Reformation, though the press was es sential for its initial spread.
grader_why: The claim's key magnitudes (43.6 pp by 1560, ≥29 pp by 1600) do not appear in the source, which instead reports 19.5 pp for 1560 and 17.3 pp for 1600; the 'no significant advantage by 1530' framing is likewise contradicted by the significant 33.2 pp main result. Because these components are unprovable from — indeed contradicted by — the cited source, the author must correct the figures, not the tool. The weakening-pattern interpretation is genuinely supported by the source but was not among the shown sentences.
fable_reasoning: Distinctive quantitative assertions contradicted by the source. Claim gives 43.6pp for 1560 and at least 29pp for 1600; Rubin reports 8.8-19.5pp for 1560 and 17.3pp for 1600. Central numbers wrong -> unsupported.
